--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


613: 			for(i2 = 0; i2 < mm2-1; i2++){


614: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1; i1++){


615: 					z1[i1] = z[i3][i2+1][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


616: 					z2[i1] = z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z[i3][i2][i1];


617: 					z3[i1] = z[i3+1][i2+1][i1] + z[i3+1][i2][i1] + z1[i1];


618: 				}


619: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


620: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1] = u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1]


621: 						+z[i3][i2][i1];


622: 					u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3][2*i2][2*i1+1]


623: 						+0.5*(z[i3][i2][i1+1]+z[i3][i2][i1]);


624: 				}


625: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


626: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1] = u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1]


627: 						+0.5 * z1[i1];


628: 					u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3][2*i2+1][2*i1+1]


629: 						+0.25*( z1[i1] + z1[i1+1] );


630: 				}


631: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


632: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1]


633: 						+0.5 * z2[i1];


634: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2][2*i1+1]


635: 						+0.25*( z2[i1] + z2[i1+1] );


636: 				}


637: 				for(i1 = 0; i1 < mm1-1; i1++){


638: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1]


639: 						+0.25* z3[i1];


640: 					u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1+1] = u[2*i3+1][2*i2+1][2*i1+1]


641: 						+0.125*( z3[i1] + z3[i1+1] );

Functioninterp(void*, int, int, int, void*, int, int, int, int)

          
Source file and linesmg.cpp:613-641

        
Modulemg_omp_cpp_g++

        

The loop is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:613-641.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Failed to get the number of paths (too many, greater than internal limit = 100000)
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is very high. Force analysis with --force-loop-streams-analysis: expect potentially high RAM/CPU footprint

Try to simplify control.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 60.17 cycles (1.69x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 53% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 81.47 cycles (1.25x speedup).

Details


57% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 51% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 52% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 63% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 80% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

    • /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:622


Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.




Conversion instructions


Detected expensive conversion instructions.

Details


  • CLTQ: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:615
    • /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:641


Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

19 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
19 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
24 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).

The binary loop is loading 2837 bytes (354 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 1004 bytes (125 double precision FP elements).

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 101.83 cycles. At this rate:

  • 14% of peak load performance is reached (27.86 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 7% of peak store performance is reached (9.86 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 72.67 cycles (1.40x speedup).

Functioninterp(void*, int, int, int, void*, int, int, int, int)

          
Source file and linesmg.cpp:613-641

        
Modulemg_omp_cpp_g++

        

The loop is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:613-641.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Failed to get the number of paths (too many, greater than internal limit = 100000)
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is very high. Force analysis with --force-loop-streams-analysis: expect potentially high RAM/CPU footprint

Try to simplify control.

      

  

    
    
      
  • gain

  • potential

  • expert
    • 
    

  

  

  


Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 60.17 cycles (1.69x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)


Vectorization


Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 53% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 81.47 cycles (1.25x speedup).

Details


57% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 51% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 52% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 63% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 80% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:

    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly:
C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)
      

    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


FMA


Detected 52 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.


  


Vector unaligned load/store instructions


Detected 26 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.


Details

Workaround


Use vector aligned instructions:

  1. align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.




Conversion instructions


Detected expensive conversion instructions.

Details


  • CLTQ: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:615
    • /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/NPB-CPP/NPB-OMP/MG/mg.cpp:641


Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


19 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
19 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
24 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).

The binary loop is composed of 205 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 153: addition or subtraction (52 inside FMA instructions)
  • 52: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 2837 bytes (354 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 1004 bytes (125 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity is 0.05 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 101.83 cycles. At this rate:

  • 14% of peak load performance is reached (27.86 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 7% of peak store performance is reached (9.86 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 101.83 to 72.67 cycles (1.40x speedup).
